title: Peanut brittle
subtitle: American hard caramel sheet candy studded with roasted peanuts
excerpt: Thin, glassy sheets of amber caramel studded with roasted peanuts. The sugar and golden syrup are cooked to the hard crack stage, the peanuts and a small pinch of bicarbonate of soda are stirred in, and the mixture is poured immediately onto an oiled tray. It sets to a glass-hard, snapping brittle within minutes. A sugar thermometer takes the guesswork out of the process. Good for gifting in a tin.
sourceNotes: Peanut brittle is an American confection documented in US household cookery literature from the nineteenth century. Fannie Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896, Project Gutenberg), gives recipes for hard sugar candies consistent with the brittle-making technique. The combination of caramelised sugar with roasted peanuts as a hard candy preparation is consistent across American confectionery literature from that period.

content.text: Have everything ready before you start: there is no pausing once the sugar reaches temperature. The
arrives quickly and the mixture must be poured and spread the moment the peanuts are stirred in. Pre-heat the baking tray in the oven at 160°C for a few minutes if you want more time to spread.

Grease and line a baking tray with baking paper. Combine the sugar, golden syrup, butter, and 2 tablespoons of water in a medium saucepan. Stir over medium heat until the sugar dissolves, then stop stirring. Clip on the sugar thermometer and cook over medium-high heat without stirring until the mixture reaches 150°C. It will turn deep amber.

Remove from the heat immediately. Stir in the vanilla, peanuts, and bicarbonate of soda; the soda will cause the mixture to foam slightly. Working very quickly, pour onto the prepared tray and tilt the tray to spread it as thin as possible, or use a greased palette knife. Leave to cool completely undisturbed, about 20 minutes. Break into shards. Store in an airtight tin with baking paper between layers.
